adjustments.notes: Reduce grammar presentation time. Increase practice on false friends and subtle register differences.

contrastivePatterns.l1Behavior: Single copula (Italian: essere, French: être, Romanian: a fi) covers both identity and state. Note: does NOT apply to Portuguese, which has the same ser/estar distinction as Spanish.
contrastivePatterns.targetContrast: Spanish distinguishes ser (identity/permanent essence) from estar (state/condition/location)

contrastivePatterns.l1Behavior: Romance cognates look identical but have diverged in meaning (e.g., Italian 'guardare', French 'assister')
contrastivePatterns.targetContrast: Many Spanish words share form with L1 cognates but differ in meaning or register

contrastivePatterns.l1Behavior: Subjunctive exists and functions similarly in most Romance languages
contrastivePatterns.targetContrast: Spanish subjunctive is obligatory in subordinate clauses after expressions of doubt, emotion, and impersonal constructions; temporal correlation rules are strict (present vs. imperfect subjunctive)

adjustments.notes: Explicit explanation of concepts not in L1. Extra ser/estar practice at every level until B2.

contrastivePatterns.l1Behavior: Single copula (to be / sein / zijn) covers both identity and state
contrastivePatterns.targetContrast: Spanish distinguishes ser (identity/permanent essence) from estar (state/condition/location/temporary quality)

contrastivePatterns.l1Behavior: Subjunctive is rare or absent in English/Scandinavian; German has subjunctive but used differently
contrastivePatterns.targetContrast: Spanish subjunctive is obligatory in subordinate clauses of doubt, volition, emotion, and impersonal constructions

contrastivePatterns.l1Behavior: English has no grammatical gender; German/Dutch gender does not always align with Spanish
contrastivePatterns.targetContrast: Spanish nouns have grammatical gender (masculine/feminine) that determines article and adjective agreement

contrastivePatterns.l1Behavior: Prepositions in Germanic languages do not map directly to Spanish equivalents
contrastivePatterns.targetContrast: Spanish prepositions (por/para, en/a, de) require explicit memorization; transfer errors are frequent

adjustments.notes: Very gradual approach. Heavy repetition. Strong visual support. CO-06 in every lesson during A1-A2.

contrastivePatterns.targetContrast: Spanish nouns have grammatical gender that governs article and adjective agreement; must be memorized with each noun

contrastivePatterns.l1Behavior: No articles (definite or indefinite) in Mandarin/Cantonese/Japanese
contrastivePatterns.targetContrast: Spanish uses el/la/los/las (definite) and un/una/unos/unas (indefinite) that agree in gender and number with the noun

contrastivePatterns.l1Behavior: Verbs do not conjugate for person, number, or tense in Mandarin/Cantonese; Japanese uses aspect markers and postpositional tense
contrastivePatterns.targetContrast: Spanish verbs conjugate for person, number, tense, and mood; each verb has a full paradigm that must be learned

contrastivePatterns.l1Behavior: No equivalent mood system in Mandarin/Cantonese/Japanese; modality expressed lexically
contrastivePatterns.targetContrast: Spanish subjunctive is a mandatory grammatical mood triggered by specific syntactic environments (doubt, emotion, volition, impersonal constructions)

adjustments.notes: Heavy emphasis on article use/omission. Preposition practice in every lesson.

contrastivePatterns.targetContrast: Spanish requires definite and indefinite articles that agree in gender and number with the noun; omission is a critical error

contrastivePatterns.l1Behavior: Slavic prepositions govern grammatical cases; Spanish prepositions govern no case
contrastivePatterns.targetContrast: Spanish prepositions must be memorized as lexical items; there are no case endings to guide choice

contrastivePatterns.l1Behavior: Single copula (быть/być/být) covers identity and state; often dropped in present tense in Russian
contrastivePatterns.targetContrast: Spanish distinguishes ser (identity/essence) from estar (state/condition); the copula is never dropped. Ser+adjective vs. estar+adjective distinctions remain challenging through B2.

adjustments.notes: Intensive phonetic discrimination for vowels. Additional writing exercises.

contrastivePatterns.l1Behavior: Arabic copula (كان) is typically omitted in present tense nominal sentences; no ser/estar distinction
contrastivePatterns.targetContrast: Spanish requires explicit ser or estar in all tenses; choice depends on identity vs. state

contrastivePatterns.l1Behavior: Arabic verbal system is root-and-pattern morphology; conjugation marks person, number, and gender but not the same tense distinctions
contrastivePatterns.targetContrast: Spanish uses a separate paradigm per tense (present, preterite, imperfect, future, conditional, subjunctive) with person/number endings

italian.additionalNotes: Critical interference on ser/estar (Italian essere covers both). Student will say '*Soy cansado'. Needs specific GR-02 and GR-04 for ser/estar from A1.

contrastivePatterns.l1Behavior: Italian 'essere' covers both ser and estar. 'Sono stanco' (I am tired) uses the same verb as 'Sono medico' (I am a doctor)
contrastivePatterns.targetContrast: In Spanish, 'Estoy cansado' (tiredness = temporary state, estar) vs. 'Soy médico' (profession = identity, ser). The choice is obligatory and systematic

contrastivePatterns.l1Behavior: Italian 'firma' means signature; 'burro' means butter; 'largo' means long
contrastivePatterns.targetContrast: Spanish 'firma' also means signature (positive transfer), but 'burro' means donkey and 'largo' means long — partial false friends require explicit attention

positiveTransfer: Ser/estar distinction exists and functions similarly to Spanish
portuguese.additionalNotes: Portuguese has the same ser/estar distinction as Spanish (positive transfer). Do not apply the romance family ser-estar contrastive note to Portuguese speakers.

contrastivePatterns.l1Behavior: Portuguese false friends are rarer than Italian but present (e.g., 'borracha' = rubber, not drunk; 'polvo' = octopus or dust depending on variant)
contrastivePatterns.targetContrast: Spanish and Portuguese share extensive cognate base; false friends require specific attention rather than systematic contrast

french.additionalNotes: Less false friend interference than Italian. Good cognate base. Watch for preposition transfer errors.

persian.additionalNotes: Persian is Indo-European, NOT Semitic like Arabic. Unlike Arabic, Persian has 6 vowels (closer to Spanish). CO-06 is less critical than for Arabic speakers. Shares some script patterns with Arabic but phonological profile is different. Treat as its own case, do not apply Arabic vowel rules.

mandarin.additionalNotes: No grammatical gender, no articles, no conjugations in L1. Everything is new. Functional approach (for expressing wishes/doubts we use this special form) rather than rule-based.

spanish.additionalNotes: Spanish is the target language being taught. Grammar constraints below enforce accuracy of generated exercises and must not be violated.

grammarConstraints.rule: present/future main clause -> present subjunctive (pueda, haga, sea); past/conditional main clause -> imperfect subjunctive (pudiera, hiciera, fuera). Example: 'No creo que [el sujeto] pueda...' NOT 'pudiera'.
